coredata.dc:title: Smartphone assisted colorimetric and fluorescent triple-channel signal sensor for ascorbic acid assay based on oxidase-like CoOOH nanoflakes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Na
Zhong, Yong Qing
Liu, Shi Gang
He, Yong Qin
Fan, Yu Zhu
Hu, Jian Hua
Mai, Xi
coredata.dc:description: Ascorbic acid (AA) is an important diet-derived antioxidant to human body. Thus, efficient and accurate detection of AA is of considerable significance in food analysis. Herein, smartphone assisted colorimetric and fluorescent triple-channel signal sensor has been developed for AA monitoring based on oxidase-like CoOOH nanoflakes. CoOOH nanoflakes can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) into reddish brown p-PDox. The carbon dots (C-dots) are further introduced, of which the fluorescence can be quenched by p-PDox. However, in the presence of AA, the CoOOH nanoflakes is reduced and thus collapsed. As a result, the oxidation of p-PD is restrained, and thus the fluorescence of C-dots keeps strong. Based on AA induced light color, low absorbance, and strong fluorescence, triple-channel signal sensor has been proposed for AA determination. The AA assay shows a dynamic response range from 0.5 to 10 μM with a detection limit of 0.09 μM. The method assay allows detection of AA in real samples such as fruit juices. Combination with portable smartphone, the developed sensor is potential for AA determination in resource-poor settings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triple-channel signal
Ascorbic acid
Smartphone
CoOOH nanoflakes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence detection of AA
Characterization of CoOOH nanoflakes and C-dots
Detection of AA in fruit juices

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of smartphone assisted colorimetric and fluorescent triple-channel signal sensor for AA detection with oxidase-like CoOOH nanoflakes. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CoOOH nanoflakes before (a) and after addition of AA (b). The inset is the pictures of CoOOH nanoflake solutions before (a) and after addition of AA (b). (B) The TEM image of CoOOH nanoflakes. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis absorption (Abs.), fluorescence excitation (Ex.), and emission (Em.) spectra of C-dots. The inset is the pictures of C-dot solution under day light (a) and 365 nm UV light (b). The TEM image (B), particle size distribution (C), and HRTEM image (D) of C-dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CoOOH nanoflakes (a), p-PD (b), CoOOH nanoflakes + p-PD (c), and CoOOH nanoflakes + p-PD + AA (d). The inset is the pictures of CoOOH nanoflakes + p-PD (a), and CoOOH nanoflakes + p-PD + AA (b). (B) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots (a), C-dots + CoOOH nanoflakes + p-PD (b), and C-dots + CoOOH nanoflakes + p-PD + AA (c). Conditions: C-dots (40 μg mL−1), CoOOH nanoflakes (7.5 μg mL−1), p-PD (600 μM), and AA (10 μM). (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of p-PDox and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of C-dots. (D) Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of C-dots before (a) and after addition of CoOOH nanoflakes and p-PD (300 μM, curve b; 600 μM, curve c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Screen captures (A) and figure analysis (B) by the ColorColl app of iPhone 6s. (C) Linear relationship of V/S versus AA concentration (0–10 μM). (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CoOOH/p-PD system with different AA concentrations (0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, and 10 μM). (E) Linear relationship of ΔA versus AA concentration (0.5–10 μM). (F) Fluorescence spectra of the CoOOH/p-PD system with different AA concentrations (0, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10, 15, and 20 μM). (G) Linear relationship of ΔF/F 0 versus AA concentration (0.5–10 μM). Conditions: C-dots (40 μg mL−1), CoOOH nanoflakes (7.5 μg mL−1) and p-PD (600 μM).

The optical responses of the sensor to AA and some common substances. The concentrations of all detected molecules are 10 μM. Conditions: C-dots (40 μg mL−1), CoOOH nanoflakes (7.5 μg mL−1) and p-PD (600 μM).
Determination of AA in fruit juice samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Smartphone assisted colorimetric and fluorescent triple-channel signal sensor for ascorbic acid assay based on oxidase-like CoOOH nanoflakes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, P.R. Chin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is an important diet-derived antioxidant to human body. Thus, efficient and accurate detection of AA is of considerable significance in food analysis. Herein, smartphone assisted colorimetric and fluorescent triple-channel signal sensor has been developed for AA monitoring based on oxidase-like CoOOH nanoflakes. CoOOH nanoflakes can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) into reddish brown p-PDox. The carbon dots (C-dots) are further introduced, of which the fluorescence can be quenched by p-PDox. However, in the presence of AA, the CoOOH nanoflakes is reduced and thus collapsed. As a result, the oxidation of p-PD is restrained, and thus the fluorescence of C-dots keeps strong. Based on AA induced light color, low absorbance, and strong fluorescence, triple-channel signal sensor has been proposed for AA determination. The AA assay shows a dynamic response range from 0.5 to 10 μM with a detection limit of 0.09 μM. The method assay allows detection of AA in real samples such as fruit juices. Combination with portable smartphone, the developed sensor is potential for AA determination in resource-poor settings.

ce:section.ce:para: As a basic vitamin for human beings, ascorbic acid (AA, vitamin C) works as a vital antioxidant during the oxidation and reduction process [1]. The AA participates in the synthesis of collagen and intercellular substance, and the lack of AA can lead to scurvy [2]. Otherwise, the over intake of AA may cause kidney calculi, urinary stone, and stomach cramps [3]. Considering that the content of AA is crucial, quantitative detection of AA is very significant in the food and pharmaceutical analysis. Many approaches have been proposed for AA monitoring, such as chromatography [4], UV–vis absorption [5,6], fluorescence [7,8], electrochemistry [9,10], and chemiluminescence [11]. Among the fluorescence methods, quantum dots were often utilized to detect AA with the help of ions [12–14]. Most of them are designed based on single signal output, which limits the accuracy target detection. Compared to single signal-based sensors, multiple sensing devices can improve the analytical accuracy as they possess more than one transducer [15,16]. Among them, portable transducer-assisted multiple sensors have become the favorable choice because of their potential application in resource-poor settings.

Smartphone is not only a commonly used tool for communication, but also a handy digital process center that can act as a transducer [17,18]. After installed a technical application (app) on them, smartphone can capture the color information and output the corresponding numerical values by the app. Therefore, combining with the software, smartphone can be applied to analyzing target-induced color change [19]. Nanomaterials-mediated colorimetric sensing system has become a hot research subject [20–22]. In particular, CoOOH nanoflakes as a kind of two-dimension (2D) nanomaterial have attracted much attention due to their unique catalysis and surface properties [23–25]. Present researches mainly focus on the quenching ability of CoOOH nanoflakes to fluorophores for AA [26] and alkaline phosphatase activity assay [27], DNA [28] and pyrophosphate detection [29], and so on. Recently, CoOOH nanoflakes have been investigated as a peroxidase and oxidase for glucose [30], alkaline phosphatase activity [31], and arsenate detection [32]. By taking advantages of their simple synthetic process and excellent oxidizability, CoOOH nanoflakes can be utilized to design a triple-channel analytical method.

Herein, smartphone assisted triple-channel signal sensor was reported for AA assay based on oxidase-like CoOOH nanoflakes. The p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) is a benzene derivative, which can be oxidized by hydrogen peroxide, oxygen, and ferricyanide. When p-PD is mixed with the CoOOH nanoflakes, the oxidase-like CoOOH nanoflakes can catalyze the oxidation of p-PD, resulting in reddish brown p-PDox (Fig. 1 ). After the introduction of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots), the fluorescence is immediately quenched by the p-PDox. However, in presence of AA, CoOOH nanoflakes are decomposed and collapsed because of the redox reaction between AA and CoOOH nanoflakes. The fluorescence of C-dots remains strong due to the absence of p-PDox. The presence of AA can lead to both color and fluorescence variation, which can be measured by smartphone, UV–vis spectrophotometry, and spectrofluorophotometer, respectively. Therefore, smartphone assisted triple-channel AA detection was developed by using oxidase-like CoOOH nanoflakes.

Ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), glucose (Gluc), sucrose (Suc), and lactose (Lac) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutamic acid (Glu) and glutamine (Gln) were provided by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). p-Phenylenediamine (p-PD) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Some other chemicals including metal salts were of analytical reagent grade. Onion and fruit juices were purchased from a local supermarket in Nanchang, China. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used in the whole experiment.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer EnSpire multimode plate reader (Boston, USA). Fluorescence measurements were conducted on a PerkinElmer LS55 spectrofluorophotometer (Boston, USA). The fluorescence emission spectra were measured from 400 to 650 nm under an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were collected on a JEOL-2100 TEM operated at 200 kV (Japan).

The CoOOH nanoflakes were synthesized based on a reported route [26]. Firstly, solutions of NaOH (250 μL, 1.0 M) and Co(NO3)2 (10 mM, 1.0 mL) were mixed thoroughly. The mixture was sonicated for 1 min and then NaClO (50 μL, 0.9 M) was added. The suspension was sonicated for another 10 min. The whole preparation process of CoOOH nanoflakes was conducted at room temperature. The obtained products were washed three times by ultracentrifugation and dispersed in 2 mL of ultrapure water. The stock solution of CoOOH nanoflakes was kept at 4 °C before use.

According to previous reports [19,33], the C-dots were synthesized from onion juice with some modifications. To obtain the onion juice, an onion was chopped into pieces and then squeezed by a juicer. The 20 mL of onion juice was transferred into a 50 mL beaker and heated by a domestic microwave oven for 6 min (power = 800 W). The dark brown product was obtained after cooled drown to room temperature. After dissolved in water, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was further dialyzed using a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1.0 kDa) for a day to remove small molecules. The synthesized C-dots were lyophilized and stored at 4 °C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence detection of AA
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 150 μL of CoOOH (0.75 mg mL−1) was dispersed in 1.0 mL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.7, 0.05 M). Thereafter, 100 μL of p-PD (9 mM) and 50 μL of different concentration of AA solution were added. The above solution was homogeneously mixed and incubated at 60 °C for 40 min. The C-dots (200 μL, 0.3 mg mL−1) were added to act as the fluorescent probes. Finally, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 380 and 470 nm) were measured, respectively.

The fruit juices purchased from the local supermarket were separately made from orange, lemon, and shaddock. The fruit juice samples (5 mL) were centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. Then, the 1 mL of supernatants was filtered through syringe filters with 0.22 μm pore size. Thereafter, colorless transparent solutions were obtained and diluted to the concentration within the detection range. Specifically, 10 μL of orange and lemon juice, and 25 μL of shaddock juice were diluted to 1 mL, respectively. The detection procedures were the same as the above AA detection method, except that the prepared juice samples were employed instead of AA solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CoOOH nanoflakes and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: As previously reported, CoOOH nanoflakes were synthesized by oxidizing and exfoliating cobalt hydroxide [26]. The synthesis procedures of CoOOH nanoflakes are relatively simple with good repeatability. The properties of CoOOH nanoflakes are confirmed by the UV–vis absorption and TEM. The UV–vis absorption spectrum shows that the maximum absorption peak is around 395 nm (curve a, Fig. 2A). The inset shows the solution is yellowish (picture a, Fig. 2A). The TEM image exhibit the two-dimension morphology of CoOOH nanoflakes (Fig. 2B). All above results confirm the successful preparation of CoOOH nanoflakes. Previous studies found that CoOOH nanoflakes can be selectively reduced by AA [26,34,35]. After addition of AA, the absorption of CoOOH nanoflakes is decreased and the corresponding solution turns near colorless (curve b and picture b, Fig. 2A). In the TEM image of CoOOH nanoflakes after adding AA, very few nanoflakes are observed due to the decomposition of CoOOH nanoflakes by AA (Fig. S1). The figures provide the research basis for AA detection with CoOOH nanoflakes.

The C-dots employed in this work were prepared from the onion juice. The optical properties and morphology of C-dots were investigated and shown in Fig. 3 . The solution of C-dots is brown under day light with sharp and shoulder absorption at 287 and 375 nm, respectively (Fig. 3A). When exposed to 365 nm UV light, the C-dot solution emits cyan fluorescence (inset, Fig. 3A). The maximum excitation and emission peaks are located at 380 and 470 nm, respectively (Fig. 3A). Additionally, the emission peaks shift from 410 to 530 nm with the excitation wavelength from 300 to 440 nm (Fig. S2). The excitation-dependent property is widely reported on fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, which can be attributed to the surface state and size effect [36–38]. The optical characters of C-dots make them promising candidates as fluorescent probes. The morphology of C-dots was investigated by TEM. The C-dots are small spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of about 3.4 nm (Fig. 3B and C). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) shows the crystallinity of the C-dots with a lattice parameter of 0.25 nm, which is corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon (Fig. 3D) [19]. Thus, compared with hydrothermally synthesized C-dots from the onion juice [19], the properties of C-dots prepared by the microwave method is similar, but the synthesis process is much more time-saving.

To evaluate the feasibility of this sensor, optical characterizations were performed, including the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence measurements. According to the previous research [31], the prepared CoOOH nanoflakes own the oxidase-like property instead of Co2+. Under the same reaction conditions, the solution of CoOOH nanoflakes or p-PD only shows negligible absorbance (curves a and b, Fig. 4A). When both CoOOH nanoflakes and p-PD are present, the strong absorbance can be observed with absorption peak centered at 490 nm (curve c, Fig. 4A). It is inferred that p-PD is oxidized to p-PDox with the help of CoOOH nanoflakes, giving raise to reddish brown products (inset a, Fig. 4A). As reported by Bandrowski in1960s [39], the structure of major oxidation product has been reported as 2,5-diamino-N,N′-bis(p-aminophenyl)-l,4-benzoquinone di-imine (p-PDox, Fig. S3A). The properties of p-PDox that obtained under different conditions were investigated. In the presence of O2, excess H2O2, or CoOOH nanoflakes, the collected solutions exhibit similar absorbance, which suggests the same main p-PDox product is obtained (Fig. S3B). However, in the presence of AA, the absorbance is quite weak and the color of solution become light (curve d and inset b, Fig. 4A). As discussed in Section 3.1, CoOOH nanoflakes can be reduced to Co2+ by AA, which greatly slows down the oxidation of p-PD. Therefore, the AA-induced shallow color and low absorbance provide bimodal detection strategy.

To propose a triple-signal sensor, fluorescence measurements were also performed for AA sensing. The sole C-dot solution emits strong fluorescence (curve a, Fig. 4B). With simultaneous addition of CoOOH nanoflakes and p-PD, the fluorescence intensity is significantly decreased, and the obtained fluorescence spectrum is blue-shifted compared with that of C-dots (curve b, Fig. 4B). We infer that p-PDox may be the quencher of fluorescent C-dots. After introduction of AA to the CoOOH nanoflakes and p-PD system, the fluorescence of C-dots remains strong due to the absence of p-PDox (curve c, Fig. 4B).

Thereafter, a series of experiments were done to explain the mechanism of fluorescence quenching and the reason of fluorescence spectrum shift. The UV–vis absorption spectra of C-dots, (CoOOH nanosheets + p-PD), and their mixture were measured. As shown in Fig. S4, the C-dot solution displays broad absorption within the visible range (curve a). The solution of CoOOH nanosheets and p-PD exhibits absorption band around 490 nm (curve b). The absorbance of C-dots obtained from curve a and that of (CoOOH nanosheets + p-PD) derived from curve b at the same wavelength were added algebraically and the sum constituted curve c. The mixture of C-dots and (CoOOH nanosheets + p-PD) was detected and the result turns out to be curve d, which is obviously different from curve c. The result indicates the formation of the complex between C-dots and (CoOOH nanosheets + p-PD), suggesting the static quenching mechanism [40,41]. Furthermore, the absorption of p-PDox overlaps with fluorescence excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 4C), suggesting that the possible quenching mechanisms may be inner filter effect (IFE) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). To further study the mechanism, the measurement of fluorescence lifetimes is performed. Compared with the decrease of the fluorescence intensity, the fluorescence lifetimes of C-dots almost keep stable with addition of (CoOOH nanosheets + p-PD) in solution (Fig. 4D), which demonstrates the mechanism of IFE and static quenching instead of FRET. In brief, the degree of color variation, absorbance decrease and fluorescence enhancement is related to the addition of AA, which provides an attractive triple-channel sensor for AA detection with CoOOH nanoflakes and p-PD system.

Experimental conditions were optimized to improve the efficiency of the AA sensor, including the concentrations of CoOOH nanoflakes and p-PD, reaction time, and pH of the buffer solution. The corresponding descriptions and figures (Fig. S5) were shown in the electronic supplementary material. The optimal detection conditions are listed as follows: CoOOH nanoflakes concentration (7.5 μg mL−1), reaction time (40 min), p-PD concentration (600 μM), and buffer solution (pH 7.7).

To develop a convenient tool for AA detection, smartphone installed by color identification app (ColorColl) was utilized. Under optimal experimental conditions, different concentrations of AA were introduced to the CoOOH nanoflakes and p-PD system. After the picture was captured and analyzed by the app, both RGB and HSV color space values were obtained. Herein, RGB and HSV are short for red (R) green (G) blue (B), and hue (H) saturation (S) value (V), respectively. The pictures captured under visible light were investigated. With increase of AA concentration, the solutions vary from deep to shallow, from dark to light (Figs. 5A, B, and S6). Thus, the HSV model was chosen to interpret the color in this assay. The obtained figures of saturation (S) are decreased, and the figures of value (V) are increased. The ratios of saturation to value (V/S) were investigated with AA concentration. As depicted in Fig. 5C, the linear relationship between V/S and AA concentration (0–10 μM) is acceptable. Then, the pictures captured under 365 nm UV light were analyzed. After the addition of AA, the fluorescence of C-dots is gradually recovered, and the solution color changed from dark to light (Fig. S7A and S7B). The figures of value (V) increase with increasing AA concentration, and show a good linear relationship with 0–5 μM AA (Fig. S7C). The smartphone-based detection method is very promising for real-time, quantitative AA analysis. Unfortunately, the smartphone sensitivity has not reached instrument-grade quality. The smartphone-based method in this work can be utilized for in-field AA testing, which can be further confirmed by colorimetric and fluorescent methods in the lab.

Thereafter, UV–vis spectrophotometry was employed to detect AA. With increasing AA concentration, obvious decrease of the absorbance is observed (Fig. 5D). The absorbance difference (ΔA, ΔA = A – A 0, A and A 0 represent the absorbance with and without AA separately) is gradually increased and shows good linear relationship with AA concentration (Fig. 5E). The detection linear range is 0.5–10 μM, and the calculated limit of detection (LOD) is 0.17 μM based on 3σ/slope (σ is the standard deviation of the blank samples). The LOD is relatively low when compared to some reported methods (Table S1). These results show that the determination of AA can be achieved with UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Fluorescence measurements were conducted to detect AA by using C-dots as fluorescent probes. The fluorescence intensity is enhanced after the introduction of AA. When the AA concentration increased from 0 to 20 μM, the obtained fluorescence emission become increasingly strong (Fig. 5F). The fluorescence intensity first increases sharply and then gradually slows down (Fig. 5G). The ratio of fluorescence difference (ΔF, ΔF = F – F 0, F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity before and after AA added, respectively) to initial fluorescence intensity (F 0) and AA concentration (0.5–10 μM) exist good linear relationship. When calculated based on 3σ/slope, the LOD of the fluorescence assay is obtained as 0.09 μΜ, which is comparable with some previous reports (Table S1).

The selectivity of the AA assay was evaluated by monitoring common molecules in fruits and vegetables, including some metal ions, sugars, amino acids, and citric acid (CA). The optical responses of the developed sensor to the analytes containing K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, glucose (Gluc), sucrose (Suc), lactose (Lac), glutamic acid (Glu), glutamine (Gln), and CA were detected. As can been seen in Fig. 6 , all analytes do not induce high absorbance difference (ΔA) or relative fluorescence ratio (ΔF/F 0) except AA. Thereafter, more potential interference factors were investigated (Fig. S8). Cysteine (Cys) has weak effect on AA detection as Cys contains a reductive sulfhydryl group (-SH). The glutathione (GSH) as a biological reducing molecule shows remarkable interference. Tea polyphenols like pyrocatechol (PC) has weak interference, whereas epicatechin (EC) exhibits heavily interfere. These data imply that the proposed method has an acceptable selectivity to AA, which may be not suitable for AA sensing in matrixes that contains high level of reducing substances such as tea polyphenols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in fruit juices
ce:section.ce:para: Fruit juices were chosen as examples to investigate the application of the AA sensor in complex system. The fruit juices were bought from local supermarket, which are orange, lemon, and shaddock juices. After pretreated, the juice samples were measured by absorption and fluorescence methods. The content of AA in these juices was listed on Table 1 . To further evaluate the accuracy of the method, recovery tests of standard addition were performed in juice samples. The obtained recoveries are in the range of 91.50–108.0% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 5%. The results of real sample assay suggest potential application of the purposed method for AA determination in food analysis.

A smartphone assisted triple-channel signal sensor has been successfully established for AA detection. By using p-PD, CoOOH nanoflakes, and C-dots, the developed sensor transduces the AA recognition into the color, absorption, and fluorescence change. The quantitative analysis of AA can be obtained directly by a smartphone, which is further confirmed by spectrophotometry and spectrofluorophotometer. The LOD of AA assay is obtained as low as 0.09 μM. The whole detection process is easy to operate and the experimental materials are cost-saving. With the merits of high sensitivity, simplicity, and triple-channel signal readout, the method is very intriguing for AA detection in resource-limited areas.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Na Li:Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Investigation.Yong Qing Zhong:Validation, Visualization.Shi Gang Liu:Methodology.Yong Qin He:Formal analysis.Yu Zhu Fan:Resources.Jian Hua Hu:Project administration.Xi Mai:Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81860610).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material: The TEM image of CoOOH with AA, fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots, the oxidation of p-PD, the absorbance of C-dots and p-PDox, optimization assay, figure analysis, selectivity assay, and comparison of AA detection methods

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118412.

Anticancer Res.

JAMA Intern. Med.

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Colloids Surf. A Physicochem. Eng. Asp.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A hybrid material composed of guanine-rich single stranded DNA and cobalt(III) oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets as a fluorescent probe for ascorbic acid via formation of a complex between G-quadruplex and thioflavin T
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.G. Liu, D. Luo, L. Han, N.B. Li, H.Q. Luo, A hybrid material composed of guanine-rich single stranded DNA and cobalt(III) oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets as a fluorescent probe for ascorbic acid via formation of a complex between G-quadruplex and thioflavin T, Microchim. Acta 186 (2019) 156.
